page.title: How to Hire a Chief Marketing Officer (CMO) in the UK
page.intro:
The Chief Marketing Officer role has evolved significantly over the last decade. Modern CMOs must combine brand strategy with performance marketing, creative thinking with data analytics, and team leadership with board-level commercial credibility. The CMO has one of the shortest average tenures in the C-suite — approximately three years — which makes getting the appointment right even more critical.

The CMO profile has bifurcated along two dimensions. The first is the balance between brand and performance: organisations with strong brand equity and long purchase cycles need a different CMO from those competing on digital acquisition and short-cycle commercial metrics. The second is the balance between internal and external orientation — some CMOs are primarily internal leaders who build marketing capability; others are externally visible brand custodians who represent the organisation in the market.

In 2026, CMOs are also expected to be literate in AI-driven marketing tools, first-party data strategy, and the privacy-first commercial models that are replacing third-party tracking. Organisations that do not include these requirements in their brief will struggle to compete for the best candidates.
considerations: Define which CMO archetype you need — brand builder, performance marketer, or integrated commercial leader — and be honest about which the business actually needs.
Clarify the marketing budget and team structure they will inherit — strong CMO candidates will assess both before accepting.
Establish whether marketing has a genuine seat at the executive table — a CMO without P&L accountability or board access cannot drive commercial strategy.
Be explicit about how marketing success is measured — attribution models, KPIs, and reporting cadence should be aligned before the brief is written.
Define the marketing-sales relationship clearly — in B2B organisations, this is frequently the most operationally important relationship the CMO will manage.
page.timeline: 10–14 weeks from briefing to accepted offer
page.whySearch: CMO is one of the hardest C-suite roles to fill well because the skill set is so broad and the market expectations have changed so rapidly. The best CMOs combine creative authority, commercial drive, and data fluency — and they are in very high demand. A headhunter with a strong commercial leadership network can identify the candidates who have genuinely delivered commercial outcomes from marketing, not just impressive campaigns.
